exercises.title: Why RAG vs fine-tuning vs prompt-only
exercises.instructions:
Match each scenario to the best AI approach (RAG, Fine-tuning, or Prompt Engineering only):

A: Make the AI always respond in formal Hindi
B: Answer questions from 10,000 pages of RBI regulations (updated monthly)
C: Make the AI always format amounts as ₹X,XX,XXX (Indian format)
D: Razorpay webhook documentation Q&A (private, updated quarterly)
E: Make the AI write in Zerodha's brand voice (friendly, jargon-free)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Formal Hindi responses → PROMPT ENGINEERING
   System prompt: 'Always respond in formal Hindi using respectful tone.'
   No docs to retrieve, no training needed — just a system instruction.
   Cost: 0 extra (just a few tokens in system prompt)

B: RBI regulations Q&A (updated monthly) → RAG
   10,000 pages: too large for prompt (token limit)
   Updated monthly: re-ingestion easy (nightly job), fine-tuning would need monthly re-training
   Private: not in AI training data anyway → RAG is the only option
   Semantic search: 'Basel III capital requirements' finds 'minimum capital ratios' sections

C: Indian number format → PROMPT ENGINEERING
   System prompt: 'Format all currency as ₹X,XX,XXX (Indian numbering system)'
   Format preference: system instruction, not document retrieval
   Cost: 0 extra

D: Razorpay webhook docs Q&A → RAG
   Private docs (not in training data)
   Quarterly updates: re-ingest pipeline re-runs on doc change
   Structured retrieval: 'retry policy' → finds webhook retry sections precisely

E: Brand voice → FINE-TUNING or PROMPT ENGINEERING
   If examples available: fine-tune on Zerodha's past communications
   If just guidelines: system prompt with examples (few-shot prompting)
   Brand voice = style → prompt-level first; fine-tune only if prompt isn't consistent enough

RULE OF THUMB:
  Frequently updated private docs → RAG
  Style/tone/format → Prompt Engineering
  Fundamental behavior change across all topics → Fine-tuning

test_cases.expected:
A: Formal Hindi responses → PROMPT ENGINEERING
   System prompt: 'Always respond in formal Hindi using respectful tone.'
   No docs to retrieve, no training needed — just a system instruction.
   Cost: 0 extra (just a few tokens in system prompt)

B: RBI regulations Q&A (updated monthly) → RAG
   10,000 pages: too large for prompt (token limit)
   Updated monthly: re-ingestion easy (nightly job), fine-tuning would need monthly re-training
   Private: not in AI training data anyway → RAG is the only option
   Semantic search: 'Basel III capital requirements' finds 'minimum capital ratios' sections

C: Indian number format → PROMPT ENGINEERING
   System prompt: 'Format all currency as ₹X,XX,XXX (Indian numbering system)'
   Format preference: system instruction, not document retrieval
   Cost: 0 extra

D: Razorpay webhook docs Q&A → RAG
   Private docs (not in training data)
   Quarterly updates: re-ingest pipeline re-runs on doc change
   Structured retrieval: 'retry policy' → finds webhook retry sections precisely

E: Brand voice → FINE-TUNING or PROMPT ENGINEERING
   If examples available: fine-tune on Zerodha's past communications
   If just guidelines: system prompt with examples (few-shot prompting)
   Brand voice = style → prompt-level first; fine-tune only if prompt isn't consistent enough

RULE OF THUMB:
  Frequently updated private docs → RAG
  Style/tone/format → Prompt Engineering
  Fundamental behavior change across all topics → Fine-tuning

hints: Updated data → RAG (re-ingest is cheap)
Private docs not in training → RAG mandatory
Style/format → system prompt (cheapest)

exercises.title: Complete the RAG similarity search call
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks in the Spring AI RAG retrieval code.

blanks.hint: vector store method for semantic search
retrieve top N most similar chunks (3-5 typical)
Document method to get the text content
prevents AI from mixing in training data

hints: similaritySearch: main vector store retrieval method
ONLY in prompt: grounds the AI in retrieved context
0.7 threshold: filters out low-relevance chunks

exercises.title: InMemoryVectorStore setup for testing
exercises.instructions:
Write a RAG test setup with InMemoryVectorStore:
1. Create 3 Documents about Razorpay webhook policy
2. Add to InMemoryVectorStore
3. Similarity search for 'retry' — should find webhook docs
4. @SpringBootTest: retrieval returns relevant documents
hints: InMemoryVectorStore: requires EmbeddingModel (Spring AI autoconfigures)
Document metadata: Map.of(key, value) — preserved through ingestion and retrieval
Semantic search: 'webhook retry policy' finds 'retried up to 5 times' (same meaning)

exercises.title: Document ingestion pipeline
exercises.instructions:
Write DocumentIngestionService:
1. Accept List<String> text chunks (simulating PDF page extraction)
2. Wrap each in Document with metadata (source, chunkIndex)
3. Add to VectorStore
4. Return ingested document count
5. @SpringBootTest: 5 chunks ingested, count returned correctly
hints: IntStream.range: create indexed documents
Map.of: metadata travels with the document through the vector store
chunkIndex + totalChunks: useful for displaying 'Source: page 3 of 12'

exercises.title: Complete RAG Q&A service
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete RAG Q&A service:
1. Ingest 4 document chunks about HDFC loan policy
2. On question: similarity search → retrieve top 2 chunks → build context prompt
3. 'Answer based ONLY on context' instruction
4. Return answer with source metadata
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): relevant question answers, irrelevant returns 'don't have info'
hints: Source in context: 'According to [Source: home-loan.pdf]...' — AI can cite sources
Empty chunks: return early with 'don't have info' (threshold filters irrelevant queries)
withSimilarityThreshold(0.5): lower for tests (InMemoryVectorStore similarity scores vary)

exercises.title: pgvector setup and Spring AI configuration
exercises.instructions:
Write pgvector Spring AI configuration:
1. application.yml: spring.ai.vectorstore.pgvector config
2. Docker Compose: postgres with pgvector extension
3. @Configuration: PgVectorStore bean setup
4. Explain: why pgvector over InMemoryVectorStore in production
hints: pgvector image: pgvector/pgvector:pg16 (pre-installed extension)
HNSW index: Hierarchical Navigable Small World — fast approximate search
Same DB: pgvector in your existing Postgres = no new infrastructure

exercises.title: Text splitter for long documents
exercises.instructions:
Write a custom TextChunker for HDFC policy PDFs:
1. chunk(text, chunkSize, overlap): split by sentences, respect size
2. Sentences not split mid-sentence
3. Overlap: include last N characters of previous chunk
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): chunk count correct, overlap present, no mid-word split
hints: Overlap: include last N chars of previous chunk → context continuity at boundaries
Sentence splitting: avoid breaking mid-sentence (embedding needs complete thoughts)
In production: use Spring AI's TokenTextSplitter (respects token limits)

exercises.title: RAG with metadata filtering
exercises.instructions:
Filter retrieved documents by metadata:
1. Documents have 'department' metadata: LOANS, CARDS, ACCOUNTS
2. Search only in the relevant department for each query
3. FilterExpressionBuilder for metadata filter
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): loans question → only LOANS docs, cards question → CARDS docs
hints: FilterExpressionBuilder: build metadata filters for the search
department='LOANS': only retrieves documents with that exact metadata value
Combine filters: b.and(b.eq('dept', 'LOANS'), b.gt('year', 2023))

exercises.title: RAG evaluation: measure retrieval accuracy
exercises.instructions:
Write a RAGEvaluator to measure retrieval quality:
1. RagTestCase: question, expectedSourceDocument, minimumSimilarityScore
2. RagEvaluator: runs N test cases, counts hits (correct doc retrieved)
3. Accuracy = hits / total
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): perfect retrieval=100%, wrong=0%
hints: RAG evaluation: measure 'did the right document come up?' before testing AI answer quality
Top-3 retrieval: hit if expected source is in ANY of the top-3 results
Accuracy threshold: aim for >80% on your test set before deploying to production

exercises.title: Chunking strategy analysis
exercises.instructions:
HDFC Bank has this policy text (200 words) about home loan eligibility. Which chunking strategy is best and why?

Strategy A: 1 chunk = entire 200-word policy
Strategy B: 50-word chunks, no overlap
Strategy C: 500-token chunks, 10% overlap
Strategy D: Split by paragraph (variable size), no overlap
Strategy E: Individual sentences (30-50 words each), 1 sentence overlap
exercises.expected_solution:
BEST STRATEGY: C — 500-token chunks with 10% overlap

WHY:
  500 tokens ≈ 375 words: provides full context for a topic
  10% overlap: 50-token overlap ensures boundary sentences appear in both chunks
  Works with most embedding models (max context: 8192 tokens)
  Token-based: respects model limits exactly
  Spring AI's TokenTextSplitter does exactly this

ANALYSIS OF EACH:
A: 1 chunk (200 words):
  - Works for small docs, but scales poorly
  - 200-page policy → 1 massive chunk → irrelevant content retrieved with relevant
  - Top-K=3 returns whole sections, not targeted paragraphs
  - Bad: retrieval precision suffers

B: 50-word chunks, no overlap:
  - Too small: loses context ('interest rate of 8.5%' — 8.5% of what? No context)
  - No overlap: sentences at boundaries split between chunks, lost context
  - Bad: both size and overlap issues

C: 500-token chunks, 10% overlap (BEST):
  - Right size: full context for a single topic/sub-section
  - Overlap: boundary content captured in adjacent chunks
  - Production-proven: used by LangChain, LlamaIndex defaults
  - Tunable: 200-800 tokens depending on document type

D: Paragraph-based (variable size):
  - Good alignment with topic boundaries
  - Problem: short paragraphs (3 sentences) = too small; long paragraphs = too large
  - Better than fixed-character splits, worse than token-based
  - Medium: situationally good for well-structured docs

E: Individual sentences:
  - Too granular: 'minimum CIBIL score is 700' loses why and context
  - High retrieval precision but low recall (splits related facts)
  - Good for FAQs with distinct Q&A pairs, bad for policy narratives

test_cases.expected:
BEST STRATEGY: C — 500-token chunks with 10% overlap

WHY:
  500 tokens ≈ 375 words: provides full context for a topic
  10% overlap: 50-token overlap ensures boundary sentences appear in both chunks
  Works with most embedding models (max context: 8192 tokens)
  Token-based: respects model limits exactly
  Spring AI's TokenTextSplitter does exactly this

ANALYSIS OF EACH:
A: 1 chunk (200 words):
  - Works for small docs, but scales poorly
  - 200-page policy → 1 massive chunk → irrelevant content retrieved with relevant
  - Top-K=3 returns whole sections, not targeted paragraphs
  - Bad: retrieval precision suffers

B: 50-word chunks, no overlap:
  - Too small: loses context ('interest rate of 8.5%' — 8.5% of what? No context)
  - No overlap: sentences at boundaries split between chunks, lost context
  - Bad: both size and overlap issues

C: 500-token chunks, 10% overlap (BEST):
  - Right size: full context for a single topic/sub-section
  - Overlap: boundary content captured in adjacent chunks
  - Production-proven: used by LangChain, LlamaIndex defaults
  - Tunable: 200-800 tokens depending on document type

D: Paragraph-based (variable size):
  - Good alignment with topic boundaries
  - Problem: short paragraphs (3 sentences) = too small; long paragraphs = too large
  - Better than fixed-character splits, worse than token-based
  - Medium: situationally good for well-structured docs

E: Individual sentences:
  - Too granular: 'minimum CIBIL score is 700' loses why and context
  - High retrieval precision but low recall (splits related facts)
  - Good for FAQs with distinct Q&A pairs, bad for policy narratives

hints: 500 tokens ≈ 375 words: sweet spot for context vs precision
Overlap: boundary sentences captured in both adjacent chunks
TokenTextSplitter: Spring AI's built-in implementation of strategy C

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay docs Q&A with citation
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay docs Q&A:
1. Ingest 5 documentation chunks with source + section metadata
2. Retrieve with similarity threshold
3. Build cited answer: 'According to [source], section [section]:...'
4. If no relevant context: 'This information is not in our documentation'
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): cited answer, out-of-scope, metadata in response
hints: CitedAnswer record: answer + sources list (separate from the text)
Out-of-scope: empty chunks → return no-citation answer
Source citation: trust from users (can verify the claim)

exercises.title: Scheduled document re-ingestion
exercises.instructions:
Build ScheduledDocumentReingestionService:
1. @Scheduled every night at 2am: re-ingest updated policy documents
2. Track last ingested timestamp per document
3. Only re-ingest documents changed since last run
4. Remove stale document embeddings before re-adding
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): scheduler exists, only changed docs re-ingested
hints: cron '0 0 2 * * *': second, minute, hour, day, month, weekday
lastModified.isAfter(lastIngested): only re-ingest if document changed since last run
delete old embeddings first: prevent duplicate/stale vectors from old version

exercises.title: Complete HDFC Bank RAG knowledge base
exercises.instructions:
Build complete HDFC Bank RAG knowledge base:
1. Ingest 6 policy documents (loans, cards, accounts)
2. Department-filtered retrieval
3. Cited answers with source references
4. 'Out of scope' detection for non-banking queries
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): loan Q, card Q, out-of-scope, citation included
hints: threshold=0.4: lenient for in-memory embeddings
KBResult.outOfScope: empty sources OR no-match message
Citations prove which documents the answer came from

exercises.title: RAG with hybrid search (keyword + semantic)
exercises.instructions:
Implement hybrid search combining keyword match + semantic similarity:
1. KeywordSearcher: exact match in document content
2. SemanticSearcher: vector similarity
3. HybridSearcher: merge results, deduplicate, re-rank by combined score
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): keyword match, semantic match, hybrid finds both
hints: Keyword: exact matches (high precision), misses synonyms
Semantic: finds paraphrases ('credit score' = 'CIBIL score'), may miss exact terms
Hybrid: best of both — keyword first (higher precision), then semantic (higher recall)

exercises.title: Complete M26 T2 RAG synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M26 T2 RAG synthesis:
1. Ingest 4 documents with metadata
2. Similarity search with threshold + metadata filter
3. Build grounded cited answer
4. RAG evaluator: measure hit rate
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): retrieval, citation, out-of-scope, evaluation
6. All 4 pass
hints: threshold=0.4: lenient for InMemoryVectorStore tests
Out-of-scope: empty docs → not grounded (citations empty)
evaluateHitRate: counts how many test queries retrieved the expected document
